The [SpyNote RAT](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0305) builder tool can be used to develop malicious apps with the malware's functionality. (Citation: Zscaler-SpyNote)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: Gooligan — [Gooligan](https://attack
output: Malware: Gooligan — [Gooligan](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0290) is a malware family that runs privilege escalation exploits on Android devices and then uses its escalated privileges to steal authentication tokens that can be used to access data from many Google applications. [Gooligan](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0290) has been described as part of the Ghost Push Android malware family. (Citation: Gooligan Citation) (Citation: Ludwig-GhostPush) (Citation: Lookout-Gooligan)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: OldBoot — [OldBoot](https://attack
output: Malware: OldBoot — [OldBoot](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0285) is an Android malware family. (Citation: HackerNews-OldBoot)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: Judy — [Judy](https://attack
output: Malware: Judy — [Judy](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0325) is auto-clicking adware that was distributed through multiple apps in the Google Play Store. (Citation: CheckPoint-Judy)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: Bread — [Bread](https://attack
output: Malware: Bread — [Bread](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0432) was a large-scale billing fraud malware family known for employing many different cloaking and obfuscation techniques in an attempt to continuously evade Google Play Store’s malware detection. 1,700 unique Bread apps were detected and removed from the Google Play Store before being downloaded by users.(Citation: Google Bread)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: FlixOnline — [FlixOnline](https://attack
output: Malware: FlixOnline — [FlixOnline](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1103) is an Android malware, first detected in early 2021, believed to target users of WhatsApp. [FlixOnline](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1103) primarily spreads via automatic replies to a device’s incoming WhatsApp messages.(Citation: checkpoint_flixonline_0421)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: GoldenEagle — [GoldenEagle](https://attack
output: Malware: GoldenEagle — [GoldenEagle](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0551) is a piece of Android malware that has been used in targeting of Uyghurs, Muslims, Tibetans, individuals in Turkey, and individuals in China. Samples have been found as early as 2012.(Citation: Lookout Uyghur Campaign)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: Allwinner — [Allwinner](https://attack
output: Malware: Allwinner — [Allwinner](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0319) is a company that supplies processors used in Android tablets and other devices. A Linux kernel distributed by [Allwinner](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0319) for use on these devices reportedly contained a backdoor. (Citation: HackerNews-Allwinner)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: Stealth Mango — [Stealth Mango](https://attack
output: Malware: Stealth Mango — [Stealth Mango](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0328) is Android malware that has reportedly been used to successfully compromise the mobile devices of government officials, members of the military, medical professionals, and civilians. The iOS malware known as [Tangelo](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0329) is believed to be from the same developer. (Citation: Lookout-StealthMango)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: Rotexy — [Rotexy](https://attack
output: Malware: Rotexy — [Rotexy](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0411) is an Android banking malware that has evolved over several years. It was originally an SMS spyware Trojan first spotted in October 2014, and since then has evolved to contain more features, including ransomware functionality.(Citation: securelist rotexy 2018)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: DroidJack — [DroidJack](https://attack
output: Malware: DroidJack — [DroidJack](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0320) is an Android remote access tool that has been observed posing as legitimate applications including the Super Mario Run and Pokemon GO games. (Citation: Zscaler-SuperMarioRun) (Citation: Proofpoint-Droidjack)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: Cerberus — [Cerberus](https://attack
output: Malware: Cerberus — [Cerberus](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0480) is a banking trojan whose usage can be rented on underground forums and marketplaces. Prior to being available to rent, the authors of [Cerberus](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0480) claim was used in private operations for two years.(Citation: Threat Fabric Cerberus)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: CarbonSteal — [CarbonSteal](https://attack
output: Malware: CarbonSteal — [CarbonSteal](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0529) is one of a family of four surveillanceware tools that share a common C2 infrastructure. [CarbonSteal](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0529) primarily deals with audio surveillance. (Citation: Lookout Uyghur Campaign)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: UNC788 (aliases: UNC788) — [UNC788](https://attack
output: Threat Group: UNC788 (aliases: UNC788) — [UNC788](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G1029) is a group of hackers from Iran that has targeted people in the Middle East.(Citation: Meta Adversarial Threat Report 2022)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Attestation — Enable remote attestation capabilities when available
output: Mitigation: Attestation — Enable remote attestation capabilities when available (such as Android SafetyNet or Samsung Knox TIMA Attestation) and prohibit devices that fail the attestation from accessing enterprise resources.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Caution with Device Administrator Access — Warn device users not to
output:
Mitigation: Caution with Device Administrator Access — Warn device users not to accept requests to grant Device Administrator access to applications without good reason.

Additionally, application vetting should include a check on whether the application requests Device Administrator access. Applications that do request Device Administrator access should be carefully scrutinized and only allowed to be used if a valid reason exists.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Interconnection Filtering — In order to mitigate Signaling System 7
output: Mitigation: Interconnection Filtering — In order to mitigate Signaling System 7 (SS7) exploitation, the Communications, Security, Reliability, and Interoperability Council (CSRIC) describes filtering interconnections between network operators to block inappropriate requests (Citation: CSRIC5-WG10-FinalReport).

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Deploy Compromised Device Detection Method — A variety of methods ex
output: Mitigation: Deploy Compromised Device Detection Method — A variety of methods exist that can be used to enable enterprises to identify compromised (e.g. rooted/jailbroken) devices, whether using security mechanisms built directly into the device, third-party mobile security applications, enterprise mobility management (EMM)/mobile device management (MDM) capabilities, or other methods. Some methods may be trivial to evade while others may be more sophisticated.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Lock Bootloader — On devices that provide the capability to unlock t
output: Mitigation: Lock Bootloader — On devices that provide the capability to unlock the bootloader (hence allowing any operating system code to be flashed onto the device), perform periodic checks to ensure that the bootloader is locked.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: System Partition Integrity — Ensure that Android devices being used
output: Mitigation: System Partition Integrity — Ensure that Android devices being used include and enable the Verified Boot capability, which cryptographically ensures the integrity of the system partition.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Antivirus/Antimalware — Mobile security products, such as Mobile Thr
output: Mitigation: Antivirus/Antimalware — Mobile security products, such as Mobile Threat Defense (MTD), offer various device-based mitigations against certain behaviors.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: User Guidance — Describes any guidance or training given to users to
output: Mitigation: User Guidance — Describes any guidance or training given to users to set particular configuration settings or avoid specific potentially risky behaviors.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Enterprise Policy — An enterprise mobility management (EMM), also kn
output: Mitigation: Enterprise Policy — An enterprise mobility management (EMM), also known as mobile device management (MDM), system can be used to provision policies to mobile devices to control aspects of their allowed behavior.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Use Recent OS Version — New mobile operating system versions bring n
output: Mitigation: Use Recent OS Version — New mobile operating system versions bring not only patches against discovered vulnerabilities but also often bring security architecture improvements that provide resilience against potential vulnerabilities or weaknesses that have not yet been discovered. They may also bring improvements that block use of observed adversary techniques.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1406
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1406.001: Steganography — Adversaries may use steganography techniques in order to prevent the detection of hidden information. Steganographic techniques can be used to hide data in digital media such as images, audio tracks, video clips, or text files. Tactics: defense-evasion.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1472: Generate Fraudulent Advertising Revenue — An adversary could
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1472: Generate Fraudulent Advertising Revenue — An adversary could seek to generate fraudulent advertising revenue from mobile devices, for example by triggering automatic clicks of advertising links without user involvement. Tactics: impact.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1466: Downgrade to Insecure Protocols — An adversary could cause t
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1466: Downgrade to Insecure Protocols — An adversary could cause the mobile device to use less secure protocols, for example by jamming frequencies used by newer protocols such as LTE and only allowing older protocols such as GSM to communicate(Citation: NIST-SP800187). Use of less secure protocols may make communication easier to eavesdrop upon or manipulate. Tactics: network-effects.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1521: Encrypted Channel — Adversaries may explicitly employ a know
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1521: Encrypted Channel — Adversaries may explicitly employ a known encryption algorithm to conceal command and control traffic rather than relying on any inherent protections provided by a communication protocol. Despite the use of a secure algorithm, these implementations may be vulnerable to reverse engineering if necessary secret keys are encoded and/or generated within malware samples/configuration files. Tactics: command-and-control.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1507: Network Information Discovery — Adversaries may use device s
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1507: Network Information Discovery — Adversaries may use device sensors to collect information about nearby networks, such as Wi-Fi and Bluetooth. Tactics: collection.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1640: Account Access Removal — Adversaries may interrupt availabil
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1640: Account Access Removal — Adversaries may interrupt availability of system and network resources by inhibiting access to accounts utilized by legitimate users. Accounts may be deleted, locked, or manipulated (ex: credentials changed) to remove access to accounts.  Tactics: impact.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1636
output:
MITRE ATT&CK T1636.003: Contact List — Adversaries may utilize standard operating system APIs to gather contact list data. On Android, this can be accomplished using the Contacts Content Provider. On iOS, this can be accomplished using the `Contacts` framework. 

 

If the device has been jailbroken or rooted, an adversary may be able to access the [Contact List](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1636/003) without the user’s knowledge or approval.  Tactics: collection.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1471: Data Encrypted for Impact — An adversary may encrypt files s
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1471: Data Encrypted for Impact — An adversary may encrypt files stored on a mobile device to prevent the user from accessing them. This may be done in order to extract monetary compensation from a victim in exchange for decryption or a decryption key (ransomware) or to render data permanently inaccessible in cases where the key is not saved or transmitted. Tactics: impact.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1636
output:
MITRE ATT&CK T1636.004: SMS Messages — Adversaries may utilize standard operating system APIs to gather SMS messages. On Android, this can be accomplished using the SMS Content Provider. iOS provides no standard API to access SMS messages. 

If the device has been jailbroken or rooted, an adversary may be able to access [SMS Messages](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1636/004) without the user’s knowledge or approval.  Tactics: collection.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1474
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1474.002: Compromise Hardware Supply Chain — Adversaries may manipulate hardware components in products prior to receipt by a final consumer for the purpose of data or system compromise. By modifying hardware or firmware in the supply chain, adversaries can insert a backdoor into consumer networks that may be difficult to detect and give the adversary a high degree of control over the system.  Tactics: initial-access.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1422
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1422.002: Wi-Fi Discovery — Adversaries may search for information about Wi-Fi networks, such as network names and passwords, on compromised systems. Adversaries may use Wi-Fi information as part of [Discovery](https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0032) or [Credential Access](https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0031) activity to support both ongoing and future campaigns.  Tactics: discovery.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1521
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1521.001: Symmetric Cryptography — Adversaries may employ a known symmetric encryption algorithm to conceal command and control traffic, rather than relying on any inherent protections provided by a communication protocol. Symmetric encryption algorithms use the same key for plaintext encryption and ciphertext decryption. Common symmetric encryption algorithms include AES, Blowfish, and RC4. Tactics: command-and-control.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1636
output:
MITRE ATT&CK T1636.001: Calendar Entries — Adversaries may utilize standard operating system APIs to gather calendar entry data. On Android, this can be accomplished using the Calendar Content Provider. On iOS, this can be accomplished using the `EventKit` framework. 

 

If the device has been jailbroken or rooted, an adversary may be able to access [Calendar Entries](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1636/001) without the user’s knowledge or approval.  Tactics: collection.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1467: Rogue Cellular Base Station — An adversary could set up a ro
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1467: Rogue Cellular Base Station — An adversary could set up a rogue cellular base station and then use it to eavesdrop on or manipulate cellular device communication. A compromised cellular femtocell could be used to carry out this technique(Citation: Computerworld-Femtocell). Tactics: network-effects.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1427: Attack PC via USB Connection — With escalated privileges, an
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1427: Attack PC via USB Connection — With escalated privileges, an adversary could program the mobile device to impersonate USB devices such as input devices (keyboard and mouse), storage devices, and/or networking devices in order to attack a physically connected PC(Citation: Wang-ExploitingUSB)(Citation: ArsTechnica-PoisonTap) This technique has been demonstrated on Android. We are unaware of any demonstrations on iOS. Tactics: lateral-movement.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1430
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1430.001: Remote Device Management Services — An adversary may use access to cloud services (e.g. Google's Android Device Manager or Apple iCloud's Find my iPhone) or to an enterprise mobility management (EMM)/mobile device management (MDM) server console to track the location of mobile devices managed by the service.(Citation: Krebs-Location)  Tactics: collection, discovery.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1474
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1474.003: Compromise Software Supply Chain — Adversaries may manipulate application software prior to receipt by a final consumer for the purpose of data or system compromise. Supply chain compromise of software can take place in a number of ways, including manipulation of the application source code, manipulation of the update/distribution mechanism for that software, or replacing compiled releases with a modified version. Tactics: initial-access.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1509: Non-Standard Port — Adversaries may generate network traffic
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1509: Non-Standard Port — Adversaries may generate network traffic using a protocol and port pairing that are typically not associated. For example, HTTPS over port 8088 or port 587 as opposed to the traditional port 443. Adversaries may make changes to the standard port used by a protocol to bypass filtering or muddle analysis/parsing of network data. Tactics: command-and-control.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK : URL Scheme Hijacking — An iOS application may be able to maliciou
output: MITRE ATT&CK : URL Scheme Hijacking — An iOS application may be able to maliciously claim a URL scheme, allowing it to intercept calls that are meant for a different application(Citation: FireEye-Masque2)(Citation: Dhanjani-URLScheme). This technique, for example, could be used to capture OAuth authorization codes(Citation: IETF-PKCE) or to phish user credentials(Citation: MobileIron-XARA). Tactics: credential-access.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK : Device Type Discovery — On Android, device type information is ac
output: MITRE ATT&CK : Device Type Discovery — On Android, device type information is accessible to apps through the android.os.Build class (Citation: Android-Build). Device information could be used to target privilege escalation exploits. Tactics: discovery.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1403: Modify Cached Executable Code — ART (the Android Runtime) co
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1403: Modify Cached Executable Code — ART (the Android Runtime) compiles optimized code on the device itself to improve performance. An adversary may be able to use escalated privileges to modify the cached code in order to hide malicious behavior. Since the code is compiled on the device, it may not receive the same level of integrity checks that are provided to code running in the system partition.(Citation: Sabanal-ART) Tactics: persistence.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1433: Access Call Log — On Android, an adversary could call standa
output:
MITRE ATT&CK T1433: Access Call Log — On Android, an adversary could call standard operating system APIs from a malicious application to gather call log data, or with escalated privileges could directly access files containing call log data.

On iOS, applications do not have access to the call log, so privilege escalation would be required in order to access the data. Tactics: collection.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1452: Manipulate App Store Rankings or Ratings — An adversary coul
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1452: Manipulate App Store Rankings or Ratings — An adversary could use access to a compromised device's credentials to attempt to manipulate app store rankings or ratings by triggering application downloads or posting fake reviews of applications. This technique likely requires privileged access (a rooted or jailbroken device). Tactics: impact.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1468: Remotely Track Device Without Authorization — An adversary w
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1468: Remotely Track Device Without Authorization — An adversary who is able to obtain unauthorized access to or misuse authorized access to cloud services (e.g. Google's Android Device Manager or Apple iCloud's Find my iPhone) or to an enterprise mobility management (EMM) / mobile device management (MDM) server console could use that access to track mobile devices.(Citation: Krebs-Location) Tactics: remote-service-effects.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1465: Rogue Wi-Fi Access Points — An adversary could set up unauth
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1465: Rogue Wi-Fi Access Points — An adversary could set up unauthorized Wi-Fi access points or compromise existing access points and, if the device connects to them, carry out network-based attacks such as eavesdropping on or modifying network communication(Citation: NIST-SP800153)(Citation: Kaspersky-DarkHotel). Tactics: network-effects.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1435: Access Calendar Entries — An adversary could call standard o
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1435: Access Calendar Entries — An adversary could call standard operating system APIs from a malicious application to gather calendar entry data, or with escalated privileges could directly access files containing calendar data. Tactics: collection.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1469: Remotely Wipe Data Without Authorization — An adversary who
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1469: Remotely Wipe Data Without Authorization — An adversary who is able to obtain unauthorized access to or misuse authorized access to cloud services (e.g. Google's Android Device Manager or Apple iCloud's Find my iPhone) or to an EMM console could use that access to wipe enrolled devices (Citation: Honan-Hacking). Tactics: remote-service-effects.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1450: Exploit SS7 to Track Device Location — An adversary could ex
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1450: Exploit SS7 to Track Device Location — An adversary could exploit signaling system vulnerabilities to track the location of mobile devices. (Citation: Engel-SS7) (Citation: Engel-SS7-2008) (Citation: 3GPP-Security) (Citation: Positive-SS7) (Citation: CSRIC5-WG10-FinalReport) Tactics: network-effects.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1432: Access Contact List — An adversary could call standard opera
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1432: Access Contact List — An adversary could call standard operating system APIs from a malicious application to gather contact list (i.e., address book) data, or with escalated privileges could directly access files containing contact list data. Tactics: collection.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1398: Boot or Logon Initialization Scripts — Adversaries may use s
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1398: Boot or Logon Initialization Scripts — Adversaries may use scripts automatically executed at boot or logon initialization to establish persistence. Initialization scripts are part of the underlying operating system and are not accessible to the user unless the device has been rooted or jailbroken.  Tactics: persistence.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1439: Eavesdrop on Insecure Network Communication — If network tra
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1439: Eavesdrop on Insecure Network Communication — If network traffic between the mobile device and remote servers is unencrypted or is encrypted in an insecure manner, then an adversary positioned on the network can eavesdrop on communication.(Citation: mHealth) Tactics: network-effects.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1436: Commonly Used Port — Adversaries may communicate over a comm
output:
MITRE ATT&CK T1436: Commonly Used Port — Adversaries may communicate over a commonly used port to bypass firewalls or network detection systems and to blend with normal network activity to avoid more detailed inspection. 

They may use commonly open ports such as

* TCP:80 (HTTP)
* TCP:443 (HTTPS)
* TCP:25 (SMTP)
* TCP/UDP:53 (DNS)

They may use the protocol associated with the port or a completely different protocol. Tactics: command-and-control, exfiltration.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1646: Exfiltration Over C2 Channel — Adversaries may steal data by
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1646: Exfiltration Over C2 Channel — Adversaries may steal data by exfiltrating it over an existing command and control channel. Stolen data is encoded into the normal communications channel using the same protocol as command and control communications. Tactics: exfiltration.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1423: Network Service Scanning — Adversaries may attempt to get a
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1423: Network Service Scanning — Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of services running on remote hosts, including those that may be vulnerable to remote software exploitation. Methods to acquire this information include port scans and vulnerability scans from the mobile device. This technique may take advantage of the mobile device's access to an internal enterprise network either through local connectivity or through a Virtual Private Network (VPN). Tactics: discovery.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1544: Ingress Tool Transfer — Adversaries may transfer tools or ot
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1544: Ingress Tool Transfer — Adversaries may transfer tools or other files from an external system onto a compromised device to facilitate follow-on actions. Files may be copied from an external adversary-controlled system through the command and control channel  or through alternate protocols with another tool such as FTP. Tactics: command-and-control.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1413: Access Sensitive Data in Device Logs — On versions of Androi
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1413: Access Sensitive Data in Device Logs — On versions of Android prior to 4.1, an adversary may use a malicious application that holds the READ_LOGS permission to obtain private keys, passwords, other credentials, or other sensitive data stored in the device's system log. On Android 4.1 and later, an adversary would need to attempt to perform an operating system privilege escalation attack to be able to access the log. Tactics: collection, credential-access.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1629: Impair Defenses — Adversaries may maliciously modify compone
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1629: Impair Defenses — Adversaries may maliciously modify components of a victim environment in order to hinder or disable defensive mechanisms. This not only involves impairing preventative defenses, such as anti-virus, but also detection capabilities that defenders can use to audit activity and identify malicious behavior. This may span both native defenses as well as supplemental capabilities installed by users or mobile endpoint administrators. Tactics: defense-evasion.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1636
output:
MITRE ATT&CK T1636.002: Call Log — Adversaries may utilize standard operating system APIs to gather call log data. On Android, this can be accomplished using the Call Log Content Provider. iOS provides no standard API to access the call log. 

 

If the device has been jailbroken or rooted, an adversary may be able to access the [Call Log](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1636/002) without the user’s knowledge or approval.  Tactics: collection.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK T1630: Indicator Removal on Host — Adversaries may delete, alter, o
output: MITRE ATT&CK T1630: Indicator Removal on Host — Adversaries may delete, alter, or hide generated artifacts on a device, including files, jailbreak status, or the malicious application itself. These actions may interfere with event collection, reporting, or other notifications used to detect intrusion activity. This may compromise the integrity of mobile security solutions by causing notable events or information to go unreported. Tactics: defense-evasion.

instruction: Explain this security concept: MITRE ATT&CK : Malicious SMS Message — Test Tactics: collection
output: MITRE ATT&CK : Malicious SMS Message — Test Tactics: collection.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Sunburst uses Image File Execution Options Injection
output: Sunburst uses Image File Execution Options Injection

instruction: Explain this security concept: Sunspot uses Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information
output: Sunspot uses Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information

instruction: Explain this security concept: Teardrop uses Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information
output: Teardrop uses Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information

instruction: Explain this security concept: Sunburst uses System Network Configuration Discovery
output: Sunburst uses System Network Configuration Discovery

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: Raindrop — [Raindrop](https://attack
output: Malware: Raindrop — [Raindrop](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0565) is a loader used by [UNC2452](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0118) that was discovered on some victim machines during investigations related to the 2020 SolarWinds cyber intrusion. It was discovered in January 2021 and was likely used since at least May 2020.(Citation: Symantec RAINDROP January 2021)(Citation: Microsoft Deep Dive Solorigate January 2021)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: Teardrop — [Teardrop](https://attack
output: Malware: Teardrop — [Teardrop](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0560) is a memory-only dropper that was discovered on some victim machines during investigations related to the 2020 SolarWinds cyber intrusion. It was likely used by [UNC2452](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0118) since at least May 2020.(Citation: FireEye SUNBURST Backdoor December 2020)(Citation: Microsoft Deep Dive Solorigate January 2021)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: Sunspot — [Sunspot](https://attack
output: Malware: Sunspot — [Sunspot](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0562) is an implant that injected the [Sunburst](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0559) backdoor into the SolarWinds Orion software update framework. It was used by [UNC2452](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0118) since at least February 2020.(Citation: CrowdStrike SUNSPOT Implant January 2021)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: Sunburst — [Sunburst](https://attack
output: Malware: Sunburst — [Sunburst](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0559) is a trojanized DLL designed to fit within the SolarWinds Orion software update framework. It was used by [UNC2452](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0118) since at least February 2020.(Citation: SolarWinds Sunburst Sunspot Update January 2021)(Citation: Microsoft Deep Dive Solorigate January 2021)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Tool: PowerSploit — [PowerSploit](https://attack
output: Tool: PowerSploit — [PowerSploit](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0194) is an open source, offensive security framework compromised of [PowerShell](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1086) modules and scripts that perform a wide range of tasks related to penetration testing such as code execution, persistence, bypassing anti-virus, recon, and exfiltration. (Citation: GitHub PowerSploit May 2012) (Citation: PowerShellMagazine PowerSploit July 2014) (Citation: PowerSploit Documentation)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Tool: HTRAN — [HTRAN](https://attack
output: Tool: HTRAN — [HTRAN](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0040) is a tool that proxies connections through intermediate hops and aids users in disguising their true geographical location. It can be used by adversaries to hide their location when interacting with the victim networks. (Citation: Operation Quantum Entanglement)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: jRAT — [jRAT](https://attack
output: Malware: jRAT — [jRAT](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0283) is a cross-platform remote access tool that was first observed in November 2017. (Citation: jRAT Symantec Aug 2018)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: gh0st — [gh0st](https://attack

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: CORESHELL — [CORESHELL](https://attack

output: Threat Group: Lotus Blossom (aliases: Lotus Blossom, Spring Dragon) — [Lotus Blossom](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0030) is a threat group that has targeted government and military organizations in Southeast Asia. (Citation: Lotus Blossom Jun 2015)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: APT33 (aliases: APT33) — [APT33](https://attack
output: Threat Group: APT33 (aliases: APT33) — [APT33](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0064) is a suspected Iranian threat group that has carried out operations since at least 2013. (Citation: FireEye APT33 Sept 2017) (Citation: FireEye APT33 Webinar Sept 2017)

output: Mitigation: Input Prompt Mitigation — Users need to be trained to know which programs ask for permission and why. Follow mitigation recommendations for [AppleScript](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1155).

output: Mitigation: Brute Force Mitigation — Set account lockout policies after a certain number of failed login attempts to prevent passwords from being guessed. Use multifactor authentication. instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Spearphishing Link Mitigation — Because this technique involves user
output: Mitigation: Spearphishing Link Mitigation — Because this technique involves user interaction on the endpoint, it's difficult to fully mitigate. However, there are potential mitigations. Users can be trained to identify social engineering techniques and spearphishing emails with malicious links. Other mitigations can take place as [User Execution](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1204) occurs.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: admin@338 (aliases: admin@338) — admin@338 is a China-based cyber
output: Threat Group: admin@338 (aliases: admin@338) — admin@338 is a China-based cyber threat group. instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: Strider (aliases: Strider, ProjectSauron) — Strider is a threat gr
output: Threat Group: Strider (aliases: Strider, ProjectSauron) — Strider is a threat group that has been active since at least 2011 and has targeted victims in Russia, China, Sweden, Belgium, Iran, and Rwanda. (Citation: Symantec Strider Blog) (Citation: Kaspersky ProjectSauron Blog)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: Stealth Falcon (aliases: Stealth Falcon) — Stealth Falcon is a thr
output: Threat Group: Stealth Falcon (aliases: Stealth Falcon) — Stealth Falcon is a threat group that has conducted targeted spyware attacks against Emirati journalists, activists, and dissidents since at least 2012. Circumstantial evidence suggests there could be a link between this group and the United Arab Emirates (UAE) government, but that has not been confirmed. (Citation: Citizen Lab Stealth Falcon May 2016)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: Sowbug (aliases: Sowbug) — Sowbug is a threat group that has condu
output:
Threat Group: Sowbug (aliases: Sowbug) — Sowbug is a threat group that has conducted targeted attacks against organizations in South America and Southeast Asia, particularly government entities, since at least 2015. (Citation: Symantec Sowbug Nov 2017)

Contributors: Alan Neville, @abnev

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: Scarlet Mimic (aliases: Scarlet Mimic) — Scarlet Mimic is a threat
output: Threat Group: Scarlet Mimic (aliases: Scarlet Mimic) — Scarlet Mimic is a threat group that has targeted minority rights activists. This group has not been directly linked to a government source, but the group's motivations appear to overlap with those of the Chinese government. While there is some overlap between IP addresses used by Scarlet Mimic and Putter Panda, it has not been concluded that the groups are the same. (Citation: Scarlet Mimic Jan 2016)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: Sandworm Team (aliases: Sandworm Team, Quedagh) — Sandworm Team is
output: Threat Group: Sandworm Team (aliases: Sandworm Team, Quedagh) — Sandworm Team is a cyber espionage group that has operated since approximately 2009 and has been attributed to Russia. (Citation: iSIGHT Sandworm 2014)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: RTM (aliases: RTM) — RTM is a cybercriminal group that has been ac
output: Threat Group: RTM (aliases: RTM) — RTM is a cybercriminal group that has been active since at least 2015 and is primarily interested in users of remote banking systems in Russia and neighboring countries. The group uses a Trojan by the same name (RTM). (Citation: ESET RTM Feb 2017)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: Putter Panda (aliases: Putter Panda, APT2, MSUpdater) — Putter Pan
output: Threat Group: Putter Panda (aliases: Putter Panda, APT2, MSUpdater) — Putter Panda is a Chinese threat group that has been attributed to Unit 61486 of the 12th Bureau of the PLA’s 3rd General Staff Department (GSD). (Citation: CrowdStrike Putter Panda)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: Poseidon Group (aliases: Poseidon Group) — Poseidon Group is a Por
output: Threat Group: Poseidon Group (aliases: Poseidon Group) — Poseidon Group is a Portuguese-speaking threat group that has been active since at least 2005. The group has a history of using information exfiltrated from victims to blackmail victim companies into contracting the Poseidon Group as a security firm. (Citation: Kaspersky Poseidon Group)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: PittyTiger (aliases: PittyTiger) — PittyTiger is a threat group be
output: Threat Group: PittyTiger (aliases: PittyTiger) — PittyTiger is a threat group believed to operate out of China that uses multiple different types of malware to maintain command and control. (Citation: Bizeul 2014) (Citation: Villeneuve 2014)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: Patchwork (aliases: Patchwork, Dropping Elephant, Chinastrats, MON
output: Threat Group: Patchwork (aliases: Patchwork, Dropping Elephant, Chinastrats, MONSOON, Operation Hangover) — Patchwork is a threat group that was first observed in December 2015. While the group has not been definitively attributed, circumstantial evidence suggests the group may be a pro-Indian or Indian entity. Much of the code used by this group was copied and pasted from online forums. (Citation: Cymmetria Patchwork) (Citation: Symantec Patchwork)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: PROMETHIUM (aliases: PROMETHIUM) — PROMETHIUM is an activity group
output: Threat Group: PROMETHIUM (aliases: PROMETHIUM) — PROMETHIUM is an activity group that has been active since at least 2012. The group conducted a campaign in May 2016 and has heavily targeted Turkish victims. PROMETHIUM has demonstrated similarity to another activity group called NEODYMIUM due to overlapping victim and campaign characteristics. (Citation: Microsoft NEODYMIUM Dec 2016) (Citation: Microsoft SIR Vol 21)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: PLATINUM (aliases: PLATINUM) — PLATINUM is an activity group that
output:
Threat Group: PLATINUM (aliases: PLATINUM) — PLATINUM is an activity group that has targeted victims since at least 2009. The group has focused on targets associated with governments and related organizations in South and Southeast Asia. (Citation: Microsoft PLATINUM April 2016)

Contributors: Ryan Becwar

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: Night Dragon (aliases: Night Dragon, Musical Chairs) — Night Drago
output: Threat Group: Night Dragon (aliases: Night Dragon, Musical Chairs) — Night Dragon is a campaign name for activity involving threat group that has conducted activity originating primarily in China. (Citation: McAfee Night Dragon) The activity from this group is also known as Musical Chairs. (Citation: Arbor Musical Chairs Feb 2018)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: MuddyWater (aliases: MuddyWater, TEMP
output: Threat Group: MuddyWater (aliases: MuddyWater, TEMP.Zagros) — MuddyWater is an Iranian threat group that has primarily targeted Middle Eastern nations. Activity from this group was previously linked to FIN7, but is believed to be a distinct group motivated by espionage. (Citation: Unit 42 MuddyWater Nov 2017)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: Molerats (aliases: Molerats, Operation Molerats, Gaza Cybergang) —
output: Threat Group: Molerats (aliases: Molerats, Operation Molerats, Gaza Cybergang) — Molerats is a politically-motivated threat group that has been operating since 2012. The group's victims have primarily been in the Middle East, Europe, and the United States. (Citation: DustySky) (Citation: DustySky)2

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: Moafee (aliases: Moafee) — Moafee is a threat group that appears t
output: Threat Group: Moafee (aliases: Moafee) — Moafee is a threat group that appears to operate from the Guandong Province of China. Due to overlapping TTPs, including similar custom tools, Moafee is thought to have a direct or indirect relationship with the threat group DragonOK. (Citation: Haq 2014)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: Magic Hound (aliases: Magic Hound, Rocket Kitten, Operation Saffro
output:
Threat Group: Magic Hound (aliases: Magic Hound, Rocket Kitten, Operation Saffron Rose, Ajax Security Team, Operation Woolen-Goldfish) — Magic Hound is an espionage campaign operating primarily in the Middle East that dates back to at least mid-2016. The group behind the campaign has primarily targeted organizations in the energy, government, and technology sectors that are either based or have business interests in Saudi Arabia. (Citation: Unit 42 Magic Hound Feb 2017)

Contributors: Bryan Lee

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: Lotus Blossom (aliases: Lotus Blossom, Spring Dragon) — Lotus Blos
output: Threat Group: Lotus Blossom (aliases: Lotus Blossom, Spring Dragon) — Lotus Blossom is a threat group that has targeted government and military organizations in Southeast Asia. (Citation: Lotus Blossom Jun 2015)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: Ke3chang (aliases: Ke3chang) — Ke3chang is a threat group attribut
output: Threat Group: Ke3chang (aliases: Ke3chang) — Ke3chang is a threat group attributed to actors operating out of China. (Citation: Villeneuve et al 2014)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: Group5 (aliases: Group5) — Group5 is a threat group with a suspect
output: Threat Group: Group5 (aliases: Group5) — Group5 is a threat group with a suspected Iranian nexus, though this attribution is not definite. The group has targeted individuals connected to the Syrian opposition via spearphishing and watering holes, normally using Syrian and Iranian themes. Group5 has used two commonly available remote access tools (RATs), njRAT and NanoCore, as well as an Android RAT, DroidJack. (Citation: Citizen Lab Group5)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: Gamaredon Group (aliases: Gamaredon Group) — Gamaredon Group is a
output: Threat Group: Gamaredon Group (aliases: Gamaredon Group) — Gamaredon Group is a threat group that has been active since at least 2013 and has targeted individuals likely involved in the Ukrainian government. (Citation: Palo Alto Gamaredon Feb 2017)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: GCMAN (aliases: GCMAN) — GCMAN is a threat group that focuses on t
output: Threat Group: GCMAN (aliases: GCMAN) — GCMAN is a threat group that focuses on targeting banks for the purpose of transferring money to e-currency services. (Citation: Securelist GCMAN)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: FIN8 (aliases: FIN8) — FIN8 is a financially motivated threat grou
output: Threat Group: FIN8 (aliases: FIN8) — FIN8 is a financially motivated threat group known to launch tailored spearphishing campaigns targeting the retail, restaurant, and hospitality industries. (Citation: FireEye Obfuscation June 2017) (Citation: FireEye Fin8 May 2016)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: FIN7 (aliases: FIN7) — FIN7 is a financially motivated threat grou
output: Threat Group: FIN7 (aliases: FIN7) — FIN7 is a financially motivated threat group that has primarily targeted the retail and hospitality sectors, often using point-of-sale malware. It is sometimes referred to as Carbanak Group, but these appear to be two groups using the same Carbanak malware and are therefore tracked separately. (Citation: FireEye FIN7 March 2017) (Citation: FireEye FIN7 April 2017)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: FIN6 (aliases: FIN6) — FIN6 is a cyber crime group that has stolen
output: Threat Group: FIN6 (aliases: FIN6) — FIN6 is a cyber crime group that has stolen payment card data and sold it for profit on underground marketplaces. This group has aggressively targeted and compromised point of sale (PoS) systems in the hospitality and retail sectors. (Citation: FireEye FIN6 April 2016)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: FIN5 (aliases: FIN5) — FIN5 is a financially motivated threat grou
output:
Threat Group: FIN5 (aliases: FIN5) — FIN5 is a financially motivated threat group that has targeted personally identifiable information and payment card information. The group has been active since at least 2008 and has targeted the restaurant, gaming, and hotel industries. The group is made up of actors who likely speak Russian. (Citation: FireEye Respond Webinar July 2017) (Citation: Mandiant FIN5 GrrCON Oct 2016) (Citation: DarkReading FireEye FIN5 Oct 2015)

Contributors: Walker Johnson

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: FIN10 (aliases: FIN10) — FIN10 is a financially motivated threat g
output: Threat Group: FIN10 (aliases: FIN10) — FIN10 is a financially motivated threat group that has targeted organizations in North America since at least 2013 through 2016. The group uses stolen data exfiltrated from victims to extort organizations. (Citation: FireEye FIN10 June 2017)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: Equation (aliases: Equation) — Equation is a sophisticated threat
output: Threat Group: Equation (aliases: Equation) — Equation is a sophisticated threat group that employs multiple remote access tools. The group is known to use zero-day exploits and has developed the capability to overwrite the firmware of hard disk drives. (Citation: Kaspersky Equation QA)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: Dust Storm (aliases: Dust Storm) — Dust Storm is a threat group th
output: Threat Group: Dust Storm (aliases: Dust Storm) — Dust Storm is a threat group that has targeted multiple industries in Japan, South Korea, the United States, Europe, and several Southeast Asian countries. (Citation: Cylance Dust Storm)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: DragonOK (aliases: DragonOK) — DragonOK is a threat group that has
output: Threat Group: DragonOK (aliases: DragonOK) — DragonOK is a threat group that has targeted Japanese organizations with phishing emails. Due to overlapping TTPs, including similar custom tools, DragonOK is thought to have a direct or indirect relationship with the threat group Moafee. (Citation: Operation Quantum Entanglement) It is known to use a variety of malware, including Sysget/HelloBridge, PlugX, PoisonIvy, FormerFirstRat, NFlog, and NewCT. (Citation: New DragonOK)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: Darkhotel (aliases: Darkhotel) — Darkhotel is a threat group that
output: Threat Group: Darkhotel (aliases: Darkhotel) — Darkhotel is a threat group that has been active since at least 2004. The group has conducted activity on hotel and business center Wi‑Fi and physical connections as well as peer-to-peer and file sharing networks. The actors have also conducted spearphishing. (Citation: Kaspersky Darkhotel)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: CopyKittens (aliases: CopyKittens) — CopyKittens is an Iranian cyb
output: Threat Group: CopyKittens (aliases: CopyKittens) — CopyKittens is an Iranian cyber espionage group that has been operating since at least 2013. It has targeted countries including Israel, Saudi Arabia, Turkey, the U.S., Jordan, and Germany. The group is responsible for the campaign known as Operation Wilted Tulip. (Citation: ClearSky CopyKittens March 2017) (Citation: ClearSky Wilted Tulip July 2017) (Citation: CopyKittens Nov 2015)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: Cleaver (aliases: Cleaver, TG-2889, Threat Group 2889) — Cleaver i
output: Threat Group: Cleaver (aliases: Cleaver, TG-2889, Threat Group 2889) — Cleaver is a threat group that has been attributed to Iranian actors and is responsible for activity tracked as Operation Cleaver. (Citation: Cylance Cleaver) Strong circumstantial evidence suggests Cleaver is linked to Threat Group 2889 (TG-2889). (Citation: Dell Threat Group 2889)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: Carbanak (aliases: Carbanak, Anunak, Carbon Spider) — Carbanak is
output:
Threat Group: Carbanak (aliases: Carbanak, Anunak, Carbon Spider) — Carbanak is a threat group that mainly targets banks. It also refers to malware of the same name (Carbanak). It is sometimes referred to as FIN7, but these appear to be two groups using the same Carbanak malware and are therefore tracked separately. (Citation: Kaspersky Carbanak) (Citation: FireEye FIN7 April 2017)

Contributors: Anastasios Pingios

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: BRONZE BUTLER (aliases: BRONZE BUTLER, REDBALDKNIGHT, Tick) — BRON
output: Threat Group: BRONZE BUTLER (aliases: BRONZE BUTLER, REDBALDKNIGHT, Tick) — BRONZE BUTLER is a cyber espionage group with likely Chinese origins that has been active since at least 2008. The group primarily targets Japanese organizations, particularly those in government, biotechnology, electronics manufacturing, and industrial chemistry. (Citation: Trend Micro Daserf Nov 2017) (Citation: Secureworks BRONZE BUTLER Oct 2017)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: APT33 (aliases: APT33) — APT33 is a suspected Iranian threat group
output: Threat Group: APT33 (aliases: APT33) — APT33 is a suspected Iranian threat group that has carried out operations since at least 2013. The group has targeted organizations across multiple industries in the United States, Saudi Arabia, and South Korea, with a particular interest in the aviation and energy sectors. (Citation: FireEye APT33 Sept 2017) (Citation: FireEye APT33 Webinar Sept 2017)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: APT32 (aliases: APT32, OceanLotus Group) — APT32 is a threat group
output: Threat Group: APT32 (aliases: APT32, OceanLotus Group) — APT32 is a threat group that has been active since at least 2014. The group has targeted multiple private sector industries as well as with foreign governments, dissidents, and journalists, and has extensively used strategic web compromises to compromise victims. The group is believed to be Vietnam-based. (Citation: FireEye APT32 May 2017) (Citation: Volexity OceanLotus Nov 2017)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: APT30 (aliases: APT30) — APT30 is a threat group suspected to be a
output: Threat Group: APT30 (aliases: APT30) — APT30 is a threat group suspected to be associated with the Chinese government. (Citation: FireEye APT30) While Naikon shares some characteristics with APT30, the two groups do not appear to be exact matches. (Citation: Baumgartner Golovkin Naikon 2015)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: APT29 (aliases: APT29, The Dukes, Cozy Bear, CozyDuke) — APT29 is
output: Threat Group: APT29 (aliases: APT29, The Dukes, Cozy Bear, CozyDuke) — APT29 is threat group that has been attributed to the Russian government and has operated since at least 2008. (Citation: F-Secure The Dukes) (Citation: GRIZZLY STEPPE JAR) This group reportedly compromised the Democratic National Committee starting in the summer of 2015. (Citation: Crowdstrike DNC June 2016)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: APT28 (aliases: APT28, Sednit, Sofacy, Pawn Storm, Fancy Bear) — A
output: Threat Group: APT28 (aliases: APT28, Sednit, Sofacy, Pawn Storm, Fancy Bear) — APT28 is a threat group that has been attributed to the Russian government. (Citation: FireEye APT28) (Citation: SecureWorks TG-4127) (Citation: FireEye APT28) January 2017 (Citation: GRIZZLY STEPPE JAR) This group reportedly compromised the Democratic National Committee in April 2016. (Citation: Crowdstrike DNC June 2016)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: APT18 (aliases: APT18, Threat Group-0416, TG-0416, Dynamite Panda)
output: Threat Group: APT18 (aliases: APT18, Threat Group-0416, TG-0416, Dynamite Panda) — APT18 is a threat group that has operated since at least 2009 and has targeted a range of industries, including technology, manufacturing, human rights groups, government, and medical. (Citation: Dell Lateral Movement)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: APT17 (aliases: APT17, Deputy Dog) — APT17 is a China-based threat
output: Threat Group: APT17 (aliases: APT17, Deputy Dog) — APT17 is a China-based threat group that has conducted network intrusions against U.S. government entities, the defense industry, law firms, information technology companies, mining companies, and non-government organizations. (Citation: FireEye APT17)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: APT16 (aliases: APT16) — APT16 is a China-based threat group that
output: Threat Group: APT16 (aliases: APT16) — APT16 is a China-based threat group that has launched spearphishing campaigns targeting Japanese and Taiwanese organizations. (Citation: FireEye EPS Awakens Part 2)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: APT12 (aliases: APT12, IXESHE, DynCalc, Numbered Panda, DNSCALC) —
output: Threat Group: APT12 (aliases: APT12, IXESHE, DynCalc, Numbered Panda, DNSCALC) — APT12 is a threat group that has been attributed to China. (Citation: Meyers Numbered Panda)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: APT1 (aliases: APT1, Comment Crew, Comment Group, Comment Panda) —
output: Threat Group: APT1 (aliases: APT1, Comment Crew, Comment Group, Comment Panda) — APT1 is a Chinese threat group that has been attributed to the 2nd Bureau of the People’s Liberation Army (PLA) General Staff Department’s (GSD) 3rd Department, commonly known by its Military Unit Cover Designator (MUCD) as Unit 61398. (Citation: Mandiant APT1)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Windows Management Instrumentation Mitigation — Disabling WMI or RPC
output: Mitigation: Windows Management Instrumentation Mitigation — Disabling WMI or RPCS may cause system instability and should be evaluated to assess the impact to a network.  By default, only administrators are allowed to connect remotely using WMI. Restrict other users who are allowed to connect, or disallow all users to connect remotely to WMI. Prevent credential overlap across systems of administrator and privileged accounts. (Citation: FireEye WMI 2015)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Spearphishing Link Mitigation — Because this technique involves user
output: Mitigation: Spearphishing Link Mitigation — Because this technique involves user interaction on the endpoint, it's difficult to fully mitigate. However, there are potential mitigations. Users can be trained to identify social engineering techniques and spearphishing emails with malicious links. Other mitigations can take place as User Execution occurs.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Signed Binary Proxy Execution Mitigation — Certain signed binaries t
output: Mitigation: Signed Binary Proxy Execution Mitigation — Certain signed binaries that can be used to execute other programs may not be necessary within a given environment. Use application whitelisting configured to block execution of these scripts if they are not required for a given system or network to prevent potential misuse by adversaries.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Setuid and Setgid Mitigation — Applications with known vulnerabiliti
output: Mitigation: Setuid and Setgid Mitigation — Applications with known vulnerabilities or known shell escapes should not have the setuid or setgid bits set to reduce potential damage if an application is compromised.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Remote Services Mitigation — Limit the number of accounts that may u
output: Mitigation: Remote Services Mitigation — Limit the number of accounts that may use remote services. Use multifactor authentication where possible. Limit the permissions for accounts that are at higher risk of compromise; for example, configure SSH so users can only run specific programs. Prevent Credential Access techniques that may allow an adversary to acquire Valid Accounts that can be used by existing services.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Regsvcs/Regasm Mitigation — Regsvcs and Regasm may not be necessary
output: Mitigation: Regsvcs/Regasm Mitigation — Regsvcs and Regasm may not be necessary within a given environment. Block execution of Regsvcs.exe and Regasm.exe if they are not required for a given system or network to prevent potential misuess by adversaries.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Registry Run Keys / Start Folder Mitigation — Identify and block pot
output: Mitigation: Registry Run Keys / Start Folder Mitigation — Identify and block potentially malicious software that may be executed through run key or startup folder persistence using whitelisting (Citation: Beechey 2010) tools like AppLocker (Citation: Windows Commands JPCERT) (Citation: NSA MS AppLocker) or Software Restriction Policies (Citation: Corio 2008) where appropriate. (Citation: TechNet Applocker vs SRP)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Password Filter DLL Mitigation — Ensure only valid password filters
output: Mitigation: Password Filter DLL Mitigation — Ensure only valid password filters are registered. Filter DLLs must be present in Windows installation directory (C:\Windows\System32\ by default) of a domain controller and/or local computer with a corresponding entry in HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\Notification Packages. (Citation: Microsoft Install Password Filter n.d)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Multi-hop Proxy Mitigation — Traffic to known anonymity networks and
output: Mitigation: Multi-hop Proxy Mitigation — Traffic to known anonymity networks and C2 infrastructure can be blocked through the use of network black and white lists. It should be noted that this kind of blocking may be circumvented by other techniques like Domain Fronting.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Mshta Mitigation — Mshta
output: Mitigation: Mshta Mitigation — Mshta.exe may not be necessary within a given environment since its functionality is tied to older versions of Internet Explorer which have reached end of life. Use application whitelisting configured to block execution of mshta.exe if it is not required for a given system or network to prevent potential misuse by adversaries.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Man in the Browser Mitigation — Since browser pivoting requires a hi
output:
Mitigation: Man in the Browser Mitigation — Since browser pivoting requires a high integrity process to launch from, restricting user permissions and addressing Privilege Escalation and Bypass User Account Control opportunities can limit the exposure to this technique. 

Close all browser sessions regularly and when they are no longer needed.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning Mitigation — Disable LLMNR and NetBIOS in loc
output:
Mitigation: LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning Mitigation — Disable LLMNR and NetBIOS in local computer security settings or by group policy if they are not needed within an environment. (Citation: ADSecurity Windows Secure Baseline)

Use host-based security software to block LLMNR/NetBIOS traffic.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Input Prompt Mitigation — Users need to be trained to know which pro
output: Mitigation: Input Prompt Mitigation — Users need to be trained to know which programs ask for permission and why. Follow mitigation recommendations for AppleScript.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Data from Information Repositories Mitigation — To mitigate adversar
output:
Mitigation: Data from Information Repositories Mitigation — To mitigate adversary access to information repositories for collection:

* Develop and publish policies that define acceptable information to be stored
* Appropriate implementation of access control mechanisms that include both authentication and appropriate authorization
* Enforce the principle of least-privilege
* Periodic privilege review of accounts
* Mitigate access to Valid Accounts that may be used to access repositories

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Data Staged Mitigation — Identify unnecessary system utilities or po
output: Mitigation: Data Staged Mitigation — Identify unnecessary system utilities or potentially malicious software that may be used to collect data from removable media, and audit and/or block them by using whitelisting (Citation: Beechey 2010) tools, like AppLocker, (Citation: Windows Commands JPCERT) (Citation: NSA MS AppLocker) or Software Restriction Policies (Citation: Corio 2008) where appropriate. (Citation: TechNet Applocker vs SRP)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Bypass User Account Control Mitigation — Remove users from the local
output:
Mitigation: Bypass User Account Control Mitigation — Remove users from the local administrator group on systems. Although UAC bypass techniques exist, it is still prudent to use the highest enforcement level for UAC when possible and mitigate bypass opportunities that exist with techniques such as DLL Search Order Hijacking. 

Check for common UAC bypass weaknesses on Windows systems to be aware of the risk posture and address issues where appropriate. (Citation: Github UACMe)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Brute Force Mitigation — Set account lockout policies after a certai
output: Mitigation: Brute Force Mitigation — Set account lockout policies after a certain number of failed login attempts to prevent passwords from being guessed. Use multifactor authentication. Follow best practices for mitigating access to Valid Accounts

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: AppleScript Mitigation — Require that all AppleScript be signed by a
output: Mitigation: AppleScript Mitigation — Require that all AppleScript be signed by a trusted developer ID before being executed - this will prevent random AppleScript code from executing.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Mitigation: Account Manipulation Mitigation — Use multifactor authentication
output:
Mitigation: Account Manipulation Mitigation — Use multifactor authentication. Follow guidelines to prevent or limit adversary access to Valid Accounts.

Protect domain controllers by ensuring proper security configuration for critical servers. Configure access controls and firewalls to limit access to these systems. Do not allow domain administrator accounts to be used for day-to-day operations that may expose them to potential adversaries on unprivileged systems.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: MacMa — [MacMa](https://attack
output: Malware: MacMa — [MacMa](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1016) is a macOS-based backdoor with a large set of functionalities to control and exfiltrate files from a compromised computer. [MacMa](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1016) has been observed in the wild since November 2021.(Citation: ESET DazzleSpy Jan 2022)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: Saint Bot — [Saint Bot](https://attack
output: Malware: Saint Bot — [Saint Bot](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1018) is a .NET downloader that has been used by [Ember Bear](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G1003) since at least March 2021.(Citation: Malwarebytes Saint Bot April 2021)(Citation: Palo Alto Unit 42 OutSteel SaintBot February 2022 )

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: BITTER (aliases: BITTER, T-APT-17) — [BITTER](https://attack
output: Threat Group: BITTER (aliases: BITTER, T-APT-17) — [BITTER](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G1002) is a suspected South Asian cyber espionage threat group that has been active since at least 2013. [BITTER](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G1002) has primarily targeted government, energy, and engineering organizations in Pakistan, China, Bangladesh, and Saudi Arabia.(Citation: Cisco Talos Bitter Bangladesh May 2022)(Citation: Forcepoint BITTER Pakistan Oct 2016)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Threat Group: Aquatic Panda — [Aquatic Panda](https://attack
output: Threat Group: Aquatic Panda — [Aquatic Panda](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0143) is a suspected China-based threat group with a dual mission of intelligence collection and industrial espionage. Active since at least May 2020, [Aquatic Panda](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0143) has primarily targeted entities in the telecommunications, technology, and government sectors.(Citation: CrowdStrike AQUATIC PANDA December 2021)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: SplatCloak — [SplatCloak](https://attack
output: Malware: SplatCloak — [SplatCloak](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1234) is a malware that disables EDR-related routines used by Windows Defender and Kaspersky to aid in evading detection.  [SplatCloak](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1234) has been deployed by [SplatDropper](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1232) and is known to be leveraged by [Mustang Panda](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0129) since 2025.(Citation: Zscaler PAKLOG CorkLog SplatCloak Splatdropper April 2025)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: StarProxy — [StarProxy](https://attack
output: Malware: StarProxy — [StarProxy](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1227) is custom malware used by [Mustang Panda](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0129) as a post-compromise tool, to enable proxying of traffic between the infected machine and other machines on the same network. (Citation: Zscaler)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: HIUPAN — [HIUPAN](https://attack
output: Malware: HIUPAN — [HIUPAN](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1230) (aka U2DiskWatch) is a is a worm that propagates through removable drives known to be leveraged by [Mustang Panda](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0129) and was first observed utilized in 2024. (Citation: 2025_IBM_PUBLOAD_TONESHELL_HIUPAN_CLAIMLOADER_MUSTANG PANDA)(Citation: Trend Micro MUSTANG PANDA PUBLOAD HIUPAN SEPTEMBER 2024)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: RIFLESPINE — [RIFLESPINE](https://attack
output: Malware: RIFLESPINE — [RIFLESPINE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1222) is a cross-platform backdoor that leverages Google Drive for file transfer and command execution.(Citation: Google Cloud Mandiant UNC3886 2024)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: MOPSLED — [MOPSLED](https://attack
output: Malware: MOPSLED — [MOPSLED](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1221) is a shellcode-based modular backdoor that has been used by China-nexus cyber espionage actors including [UNC3886](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G1048) and [APT41](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0096).(Citation: Google Cloud Mandiant UNC3886 2024)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: REPTILE — [REPTILE](https://attack
output: Malware: REPTILE — [REPTILE](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1219) is an open-source Linux rootkit with multiple components that provides backdoor access and functionality.(Citation: Google Cloud Mandiant UNC3886 2024)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: CLAIMLOADER — [CLAIMLOADER](https://attack
output: Malware: CLAIMLOADER — [CLAIMLOADER](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1236) is a malware variant that frequently accompanies legitimate executables that are used for DLL side-loading known to be leveraged by [Mustang Panda](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0129) and was first observed utilized in 2021.(Citation: IBM MUSTANG PANDA PUBLOAD CLAIMLOADER JUNE 2025)(Citation: 2025_IBM_PUBLOAD_TONESHELL_HIUPAN_CLAIMLOADER_MUSTANG PANDA)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: THINCRUST — [THINCRUST](https://attack
output: Malware: THINCRUST — [THINCRUST](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1223) is a Python-based backdoor tool that has been used by [UNC3886](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G1048) since at least 2023.(Citation: Mandiant Fortinet Zero Day)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: BOOKWORM — [BOOKWORM](https://attack
output: Malware: BOOKWORM — [BOOKWORM](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1226) is a modular trojan known to be leveraged by [Mustang Panda](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0129) and was first observed utilized in 2015.  [BOOKWORM](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1226) was later updated in late 2021 and the fall of 2022 to launch shellcode represented as UUID parameters. (Citation: Broadcom)(Citation: Unit42 Bookworm Nov2015)(Citation: Palo Alto Networks, Unit 42)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: TONESHELL — [TONESHELL](https://attack
output: Malware: TONESHELL — [TONESHELL](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1239) is a custom backdoor that has been used since at least Q1 2021.(Citation: Palo Alto Unit42 STATELY TAURUS TONESHELL September 2023)   [TONESHELL](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1239) malware has previously been leveraged by Chinese affiliated actors identified as [Mustang Panda](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0129).(Citation: ATTACKIQ MUSTANG PANDA TONESHELL March 2023)(Citation: Zscaler)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: Havoc — [Havoc](https://attack
output: Malware: Havoc — [Havoc](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1229) is an open-source post-exploitation command and control (C2) framework first released on GitHub in October 2022 by C5pider (Paul Ungur), who continues to maintain and develop it with community contributors. [Havoc](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1229) provides a wide range of offensive security capabilities and has been adopted by multiple threat actors to establish and maintain control over compromised systems.

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: CASTLETAP — [CASTLETAP](https://attack
output: Malware: CASTLETAP — [CASTLETAP](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1224) is an ICMP port knocking backdoor that has been installed on compromised FortiGate firewalls by [UNC3886](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G1048).(Citation: Mandiant Fortinet Zero Day)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Malware: MEDUSA — [MEDUSA](https://attack
output: Malware: MEDUSA — [MEDUSA](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1220) is an open-source rootkit that is capable of dynamic linker hijacking, command execution, and logging credentials.(Citation: Google Cloud Mandiant UNC3886 2024)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Tool: Quick Assist — [Quick Assist](https://attack
output: Tool: Quick Assist — [Quick Assist](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1209) is a remote assistance tool primarily for Microsoft Windows, although a macOS version also exists. [Quick Assist](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1209) allows for remote screen sharing and, with end user approval, remote control and command execution on the enabling device.(Citation: Microsoft Storm-1811 2024)(Citation: Microsoft Quick Assist 2024)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Tool: cipher
output: Tool: cipher.exe — [cipher.exe](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1205) is a native Microsoft utility that manages encryption of directories and files on NTFS (New Technology File System) partitions by using the Encrypting File System (EFS).(Citation: cipher.exe)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Tool: attrib — [attrib](https://attack
output: Tool: attrib — [attrib](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S1176) is a Windows utility used to display, set or remove attributes assigned to files or directories.(Citation: Microsoft attrib 2023)

instruction: Explain this security concept: Tool: Sliver — [Sliver](https://attack
output: Tool: Sliver — [Sliver](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0633) is an open source, cross-platform, red team command and control (C2) framework written in Golang. [Sliver](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0633) includes its own package manager, "armory," for staging and downloading additional tools and payloads to the primary C2 framework.(Citation: Bishop Fox Sliver Framework August 2019)(Citation: Cybereason Sliver Undated)
